brief.message: Hey! I'm heading off on leave starting Monday. Could you draft a quick out-of-office auto-reply for me? I'll be back on Monday the 6th — and if anything's urgent, people should reach out to Darren.

Keep the total document under 150 words, and make sure the tone stays blameless throughout — no finger-pointing or any references to specific individuals.

brief.message: We had a 3-hour production outage yesterday and I need to send a post-mortem summary to the wider eng team. Can you draft the email? It needs to hit the right tone and cover the key points — I'll review it before it goes out.

criteria.text: Opens with a single sentence stating the root cause
Contains exactly 3 numbered action items
Each action item names a responsible owner or team

4.messyPrompt: So I need you to write up a post-incident email summary following our server outage. One more thing — please make sure the overall tone stays blameless throughout the whole email, keeping the focus on systems and processes rather than pointing fingers at or blaming any specific individuals. Thanks so much!

brief.message: We had a four-hour database outage yesterday and I need a clean email drafted to go out to all stakeholders — board, sales, customer success. It needs to explain what happened, what went wrong, what we're doing about it with clear owners, and close professionally. Most of these readers aren't technical so keep it accessible.

criteria.text: Opens with exactly one sentence summarising the incident (system affected and duration), with no salutation or greeting.
Lists exactly 3 root causes in a numbered list.
Proposes exactly 2 remediation actions, each specifying a named responsible party.
Closes with a sincere apology of no more than 2 sentences — empathetic but not excessive.
Uses no technical jargon — language must be accessible to non-technical readers throughout.

4.messyPrompt: Can you write a professional post-mortem email to be sent to non-technical stakeholders following a four-hour database outage that occurred yesterday? I'd like the email to start with a single summarising sentence covering what went down and for how long — and please skip any salutation or greeting at the top. Then list exactly three root causes in a numbered format. Include two remediation actions, each with a clear owner named. brief.message: We've got reports spreading on social about a safety issue with the BlendPro 3000. Legal says no admissions of fault yet, but silence is making it worse. Need a holding statement we can post today — max three sentences, acknowledges the reports, mentions we're investigating, and gives customers somewhere to turn. States that an investigation is currently underway
Includes a customer contact channel (e.g. helpline, email, or support link)
No more than three sentences in total
Empathetic, non-defensive tone with no preamble — statement begins immediately

Also make sure to mention that an investigation is currently underway, and include some way for customers to contact us with their concerns. Keep the whole thing to no more than three sentences total, and make the tone feel empathetic and caring rather than defensive or dismissive. No introduction or preamble — just go straight into the statement.

brief.message: We had a production outage over the weekend — checkout was down for about 40 minutes. I need a concise handover note I can forward straight to the VP and all department heads. They're non-technical, so plain language only, and I want it action-focused with clear ownership on the fix.

brief.message: Legal's given us the green light to raise subscription prices by 15%, effective 1 September. brief.message: Legal have put a hold on us disclosing any severance figures, so nothing about pay-outs in this draft. Contains no mention of severance, compensation, or any financial pay-out.

5.messyPrompt: Please write an email for employees who are being informed that they are being made redundant — the tone should feel really empathetic and human, definitely not cold or clinical in any way. Please make sure the email is structured into exactly three paragraphs, not two and not four, exactly three. brief.message: Our payment gateway has been down for 6 hours and the vendor's support team isn't moving fast enough. I need something that goes straight to their leadership — makes clear we're serious, spells out the damage we're taking, gives them a hard deadline, but doesn't tip into threats. Can you draft it?

5.messyPrompt: I need you to write an escalation email to our payment gateway vendor — the system has been down and honestly the regular support team just isn't responding quickly enough for us. Could you make sure to name the system and also mention how long it has actually been offline? Also please include some specific details about the kind of business impact we're experiencing, like blocked transactions or revenue we're losing. brief.message: We had that service outage yesterday — 4 hours and 12 minutes of downtime, running from 09:14 to 13:26 UTC. I need a customer-facing email drafted. Apologise, but only the once — I don't want the whole thing dripping with sorrys. Mention the exact duration. Give exactly 3 bullets on what we're doing to prevent a repeat. Keep the technical bits in plain English, no jargon. And no passive-voice blame-deflecting — none of that 'mistakes were made' stuff. Can you sort it?

criteria.text: Contains exactly one apology — no repetition of sorry or apologetic phrasing anywhere else in the email
States the outage duration as 4 hours 12 minutes or the specific times 09:14–13:26 UTC
Lists exactly 3 preventive measures in bullet-point format
All technical content written in plain English with no jargon
Written entirely in active voice with no passive constructions or blame-deflecting language

5.messyPrompt: I need you to write a customer-facing email about the outage we experienced. Please include an apology somewhere, but I only want you to apologise one single time — don't keep repeating sorry throughout the email or it'll come across as grovelling. The outage lasted 4 hours and 12 minutes in total, running from 09:14 UTC all the way through to 13:26 UTC, so please make sure you mention that somewhere in the email. I'd also like you to include exactly 3 bullet points that explain the three concrete steps we're taking to make sure this kind of thing doesn't happen again. Please keep all of the technical content written in plain, simple English — absolutely no jargon or technical terms that customers won't understand. And finally, please write the whole thing in active voice — I really don't want any of that wishy-washy passive 'mistakes were made' blame-deflecting kind of language anywhere in it.

brief.message: We had a database outage Friday night and I need a clean post-mortem drafted before Monday's engineering review. I've got specific requirements around structure, timeline format, tone, and word choices — can you help pull something together?

criteria.text: Structured into exactly four sections in this order: Timeline, Root Cause, Impact, Action Items
Timeline entries are listed chronologically, each prefixed with a timestamp in HH:MM format
Tone is neutral and blameless — no blame attributed to any individual or team
Each action item names a responsible owner and a target deadline
The words 'failure' and 'fault' do not appear anywhere in the document

5.messyPrompt: Could you write a post-mortem report for me? I need it to have exactly four sections, and those sections need to appear in this specific order: Timeline first, then Root Cause, then Impact, and finally Action Items last. For the Timeline section, please make sure all events are listed in chronological order and that each event has a timestamp written in HH:MM format right next to it so it's clear when things happened. It's also really important that the tone throughout the whole document stays completely neutral and blameless — meaning you should not assign blame or fault to any individual person or any team at all. For the Action Items section, every single action item must clearly include the name of the owner who is responsible for it and a specific target deadline. Oh, and one more thing: please don't use the words 'failure' or 'fault' anywhere at all in the document.

brief.message: Hi — CEO needs talking points for Thursday's Q3 earnings call. Legal won't sign off on real figures until tomorrow, so nothing concrete can go in yet — use placeholders wherever a number or percentage would appear. Structure it as three sections: Revenue, Outlook, Q&A Bridge. Each section should be two or three sentences max, written in his voice (first person). Confident and forward-looking throughout — absolutely no hedging. Can you draft this?

criteria.text: Contains exactly three sections with the headings Revenue, Outlook, and Q&A Bridge
Each section is exactly 2–3 sentences long (no more, no fewer)
Written throughout in first-person singular as the CEO (uses 'I', not 'we' or third person)
All financial figures and percentages are replaced with bracketed placeholders such as [X]% or [figure] — no real numbers appear
Tone is confident and forward-looking with zero hedging words (no 'might', 'could', 'hopefully', 'may')

5.messyPrompt: I need you to write a script for our CEO to use during the Q3 earnings call happening on Thursday. Please break it into three clearly labelled sections, which should be Revenue, then Outlook, and then a Q&A Bridge section. Each one of those three sections should be exactly two to three sentences long — not one sentence, not four, just two or three sentences per section. The whole thing needs to be written in first person as if the CEO himself is speaking, so please use 'I' throughout and avoid 'we' or any third-person references to the CEO. Since our legal team hasn't cleared any real numbers yet, wherever there would be a specific financial figure or a percentage, please replace it with a placeholder like [X]% or [figure] rather than including any actual numbers. Finally, the tone throughout the whole script should feel confident and forward-looking — please avoid any uncertain or hedging language such as 'might', 'could', 'may', or 'hopefully'.
